Initiated
Exhibit · 2025
Velosidy Enterprise debt collection Communication governance

Send is not a button.
It’s a liability event.

In debt collection, a single unvetted email can mean a regulatory violation, a lawsuit, or a lost license. I redesigned Velosidy’s “Send Communication” from a one-click action into a permission-gated lifecycle, improving review velocity and audit visibility without grinding agents to a halt.

▶ Run the system, 60-sec simulator Read the case study ↓
Team
1 Product Designer (me), Business Analyst, Product Manager, Engineering
My role
End-to-end product design, research, flows, UI, and handoff
Duration
~2 months, shipped to production
The constraint
Every outbound message is regulated. Wrong content, wrong channel, or wrong timing = compliance exposure.
State 01 · Initiated, the risk

One unvetted email is all it takes

Velosidy is a debt-collection platform that manages consumer accounts, communications, and compliance across multiple outbound channels, agency letters, vendor-dispatched mail, email, and in-house print.

During discovery, one pattern kept surfacing: outbound communication was treated as a casual action. Any agent could compose and dispatch a message in seconds. There was no manual validation before send, no enforced review for high-risk channels, and no single place to see what had gone out, what was pending, and what had bounced back.

What failure actually looks like

An agent sends a balance letter to a consumer whose account is under dispute. The required validation notice isn’t attached. In a regulated collection environment, that’s not a typo, it’s a documented violation the company can be fined or sued over, and the agency’s license is the collateral. The send button was the cheapest action in the product carrying the most expensive consequence.

The risk concentrated in four places: agents could initiate without configuration guardrails, vendor communications dispatched without document review, companion documents were never validated for completeness, and nobody could see the full lifecycle of a message after it left. The brief I wrote for myself: make wrong sends structurally difficult, not procedurally discouraged.

State 02 · In Review, the reframe

Modeling send as a lifecycle

The obvious fix is a confirmation dialog on the send button. But people learn to click through those in a week, so the change had to be structural, not a warning.

A message is an object that moves through states. Every transition between them is a place a check or an approval can sit.

Once you model it that way, the design questions get concrete: which transitions need a human gate, which need an automated check, who owns each state, and where it all becomes visible.

Initiated→ Review · gated→ Dispatched→ Returned / Closed

Three rules fell out of that model. Catch bad sends at creation, while the agent is still choosing channel, template, and recipient, because a fix there costs seconds and a fix after dispatch costs a lawyer. Make review a place a message waits, not a pop-up it clicks past: high-risk messages sit in the queue until someone with authority moves them. And calibrate the gate to the damage, so vendor mail, where a third party prints in the client’s name, gets the heaviest one while in-house drafts stay fast.

State 03 · Gated, the system

Five flows, one state machine

Each flow below is presented the way it was designed: the tension it had to resolve, the decision, why that decision over the alternative, and the screen as evidence. Every one traces back to a principle, and under every screen, a craft notes panel justifies the screen-level UI decisions, including what each layout choice beat. But before the screens, run the system yourself.

Send Simulator, 60 seconds
Initiated→ Review→ Dispatched→ Returned→ Closed
Audit trail
    Recording
    Zoom out, why the flow itself

    The architecture under the screens

    Strip away the screens and what remains is the UX architecture: one entry point, stages ordered by consequence, commitment that increases as the steps progress, and an ending that is always a named state. Each of those is a deliberate choice, and each had an alternative.

    One entry point. Before, sending was several unrelated flows, “print letter” lived in one place, vendor exports in another, each with its own logic and its own gaps. The redesign collapses everything into a single Send Communication action whose very first question, In-House or Vendor?, is the risk fork. Agents learn one shape; the system varies the gates behind it. The alternative (keep per-channel flows, harden each separately) would have meant training agents on N tools and auditing N paths forever.

    Stages ordered by consequence. The most consequential decision comes first and the irreversible act comes last. Type scopes which templates exist; template scopes channels and companion requirements; documents are constrained at the point of attachment. Because each stage narrows the next, an invalid communication is never assembled, it’s unreachable. The flow front-loads decisions and back-loads commitment.

    Reversal stays free until the end. Back and Cancel live on every step; nothing costs anything until dispatch. By the time the flow reaches the only action that can’t be undone, every input has been constrained, previewed, and, on the vendor path, handed to a second human. Friction concentrates at exactly one point instead of being smeared across the journey.

    No path ends in a void. Printed, queued, vendored, rejected, or returned, every communication terminates in a named Mail Manager state with an owner and a next action. “Send” stopped being the end of the agent’s knowledge and became the beginning of the system’s.

    What this buys the agent

    Governance systems get described as protecting the company. This flow was equally designed to protect, and speed up, the person clicking.

    Speed

    No guessing on a live call

    The flow only ever asks valid questions: eligible templates, allowed channels, permitted documents. An agent on a live call never stops to wonder whether a combination is compliant. The system has already done that thinking; the agent supplies intent. Fewer rejections also means fewer rework loops eating the afternoon.

    Safety

    A misclick can’t cause a violation

    Every action is attributed, and every high-risk dispatch passes a second pair of eyes. One agent can’t single-handedly send something non-compliant, so the gate protects the person as much as the company. The audit trail that covers the business is the same one that proves the agent did everything right.

    Visibility

    Answers without asking around

    “Did that letter go out? Did it bounce? Who approved it?” used to be a walk to somebody’s desk. Now it’s a Mail Manager lookup, status, address, documents, and history on one row. Agents answer consumers and attorneys from the screen, not from memory.

    Learning

    Learn the shape once

    In-house, vendor, email, letter, text, every channel runs the same staged flow with different gates. A new agent who learns one path is productive on all of them, because nothing about the geography ever changes. Onboarding teaches a model, not a manual.

    P1

    Guided communication initiation

    Tension
    Agents need to initiate fast, but free-form initiation is exactly where wrong channels, wrong templates, and missing context enter the system.
    Decision
    A structured configurator: the agent picks the delivery type (In-House or Vendor), then a template from only the pre-configured, eligible set, then a channel (Email, Letter, Text), and verifies a compiled preview before moving forward.
    Why
    Structure at initiation prevents early-stage mistakes instead of detecting them later. The agent can’t configure an invalid communication, so downstream review starts from a legal baseline.
    Rejected: free compose + validation on submit

    Letting agents compose freely and validating at submit was faster to build, but it turns every error into rework and trains agents to treat warnings as noise. Catching errors before they exist beats catching them after; the configurator made the invalid state unreachable rather than merely flagged.

    1. A modal over the account, not a separate page. The choice of what to send depends on live account context, balance, status, dispute flags. A full-page wizard would sever that context; the modal keeps it one glance away.
    2. Radio buttons for Type and Channel; a dropdown for Template. Type and channel are risk forks, every option must be visible so the In-House/Vendor decision is conscious, not defaulted. Templates are a long, tenant-configurable content list, which is exactly what dropdowns are for.
    3. The preview lives inside the same step. A separate preview screen is the first thing skipped under call-time pressure. Inline preview makes verification free instead of optional.
    4. The primary button says Next, not Send. Labels tell the truth about the flow: configuration is the first state of a lifecycle, and the UI never promises a dispatch it can't yet allow.
    P2 · P3

    Review-gated vendor communication

    Tension
    Vendor mail is the highest-liability channel, a third party physically dispatches documents in the client’s name, yet it was the channel with the least oversight.
    Decision
    A hard permission gate: for vendor communications the final action is not “Send”, it’s “Submit for Review.” The package routes to a supervisor review queue, and only an explicit, attributed approval releases it to the vendor.
    Why
    Once a vendor prints and mails, there is no recall. For irreversible channels, prevention is the only control that works, so this is where the system spends its largest friction budget.
    Rejected: post-hoc audit sampling

    Compliance teams often audit a sample of sent mail after the fact. I rejected this because a violation discovered after dispatch is still a violation, auditing measures the damage, gating prevents it. Sampling also creates a perverse incentive: most mistakes are never seen.

    1. A read-only summary instead of editable fields. Restating the agent's choices as plain text forces a re-read, confirmation by comprehension, rather than re-scanning controls they've already half-stopped seeing.
    2. Companion documents come after configuration, not before. The sequence mirrors the legal logic: the primary communication defines which companions are required.
    3. Thumbnails with file size and a remove affordance. Wrong-file errors are visual errors, a filename list can't catch the same report attached twice; thumbnails can.
    4. The primary action is named Submit for Review. An honest label is itself a control: agents who know a human will read this package prepare it differently than agents who think they're firing into the void.
    P1

    Built-in companion document validation

    Tension
    Many communications are only compliant with their companions, validation notices, disclosures, account statements. A perfect letter with a missing attachment is still a violation.
    Decision
    Companion documents become a governed, first-class step: eligibility is set at the template level (“Allow Companion Documents”), and attachment happens through a controlled picker, up to 5 files, PDF only, selected from the account’s own document folders rather than arbitrary uploads.
    Why
    Attachment errors are silent, nothing in a letter’s content reveals what’s missing or wrong alongside it. Constraining format, quantity, and source at the point of attachment keeps invalid documentation from ever entering the workflow, and ties every attached file back to a traceable record on the account.
    Rejected: a checklist for the agent

    A visible checklist still relies on a human confirming under time pressure, and checklists decay into ritual. Machine-verifiable requirements should be machine-verified; human judgment is saved for the review gate, where it’s genuinely needed.

    1. A picker over the account's own folders, not an OS file upload. Chain of custody: every attachment traces to a record already in the system, not to a file on somebody's desktop.
    2. Constraints announced up front and enforced live. “Up to 5 files, PDF only” sits in a banner before the first click, preventing the error beats reporting it after the agent has hunted for files.
    3. A metadata table, not a thumbnail grid. Choosing evidence needs provenance, who modified it, when, how big, more than it needs pictures.
    4. The confirm button counts: Add Selected (5). Continuous feedback makes the quantity limit self-policing.
    P3

    In-house communication flow

    Tension
    Not everything leaves through a vendor. In-house print-and-mail needed to exist inside the same governed lifecycle without inheriting the vendor channel’s heavy gate.
    Decision
    In-house communications end in a controlled action hierarchy, Cancel, Print Now, or Send to Mail Queue, instead of a single send button. Same states, same visibility, lighter gate calibrated to the channel’s lower irreversibility.
    Why
    If governance is uniform regardless of risk, people route around it. Calibrating friction to liability keeps the system honest: the heavy gate stays credible because it isn’t everywhere.
    1. Three explicit actions, weighted by visual hierarchy. Cancel is quiet, Print Now is an outline, Send to Mail Queue is the filled primary, the styling encodes the organization's preference: the governed queue is the default path, immediate print the exception.
    2. No unified Send button. A single send hides a routing decision inside it. Splitting it makes the agent choose the path consciously, and ambiguity is where mistakes live.
    3. The same modal skeleton as the vendor flow. One learned shape, different gates. Agents shouldn't pay a new learning cost per channel, only the gate changes, never the geography.
    P2

    Mail Manager, the lifecycle, made visible

    Tension
    Gates without visibility just relocate the problem: reviewers need to see what’s waiting, supervisors need to see what went out, and returned mail needs an owner.
    Decision
    A centralized Mail Manager that organizes every communication by its lifecycle state, In Review, Manual, Sent, Vendor, Returned, replacing fragmented, channel-by-channel views.
    Why
    The state machine is only governable if it’s observable. Organizing by state (not by channel or date) means each queue is also a worklist: every item visible in a tab is an item someone is accountable for moving.
    1. Cards with one-line job descriptions, not a nav list. The dashboard doubles as the training document, a new supervisor learns the entire state machine from this screen, no manual required.
    2. Tiles named by lifecycle state, not by channel or team. The system's organizing principle repeats at every level, so navigation itself reinforces the mental model.
    3. Deliberately flat at v1, no counts, charts, or KPIs on the tiles. The first job of this screen was teaching the states. Operational density is earned after literacy, not before.

    In Review, where authority lives

    Mail and companion documentation that needs approval is routed here before dispatch, In-House and Vendor in separate tabs, each item carrying its status (IN-REVIEW, INIT, PRINT), creator, and companion-document count. Reviewers see the full package, and every approval is an attributed act. This queue is the governance layer; everything else feeds it or drains it.

    1. In-House / Vendor counts in the header. Supervisors triage by liability, the vendor queue announces its load before a single click.
    2. A familiar table with a status column, not a kanban board. Reviewers live in tables all day. Reusing the grammar of every other queue made review feel like work they already knew, not a new tool to resent.
    3. Companion-doc count as a scannable column. Completeness is visible at row level, a reviewer can spot a zero-attachment validation letter without opening anything.

    Manual, tracked even when handled by hand

    Operational reality includes mail that must be manually printed and sent. Rather than letting it escape the system, Manual Mail gives it tracked statuses (INIT, PRINT), visible companion-document counts, and controlled template actions, print, view, delete, so even hand-processing produces an audit trail.

    1. Manual doesn't mean untracked. INIT and PRINT statuses give hand-processing the same state grammar as every automated path.
    2. Row actions limited to Print / View / Delete. You cannot approve from here, separation of duties is enforced by what the menu simply doesn't contain.

    Sent, the audit trail by default

    Every dispatched communication lands here, In-House and Vendor in separate tabs, with status (EMAILED, MAILED), address, creator, and documents attached. When a regulator or attorney asks “what exactly was sent, when, and by whom,” the answer is a filter, not an investigation. “Mark as returned” closes the loop into the Returned queue.

    1. The dispatch address surfaced as columns. Returned mail is usually an address problem. Showing exactly where each item went makes “Mark as returned” a verifiable act, not a guess.
    2. Mark as returned lives in the row menu. Failures observed by humans, a letter physically back on a desk, need a one-click path into the governed lifecycle, or they leak out of it.

    Vendor, third-party dispatch under watch

    Mail queued for vendor handoff keeps its own state view so the highest-liability channel never blends into the noise. Items sit in INIT with their companion-document counts until the vendor processes them, the visibility gap that used to exist between “sent to vendor” and “actually dispatched” is now a tracked state.

    1. A read-mostly table. Once queued for a third party, items can be viewed or withdrawn, not edited. Mutating a package mid-handoff is exactly the ambiguity the gate exists to prevent.
    2. INIT as a visible holding state. The gap between “we queued it” and “the vendor ran it” used to be invisible. Now it's a named place someone owns.

    Returned, failure gets an owner

    Returned mail is a compliance signal, not a dead letter: a bad address can invalidate notice requirements. The Returned queue tracks each item’s returned address, whether an updated address exists, and who marked it returned (system or agent), turning failure into a state with a correction path instead of an end.

    1. Columns built around the corrective question. Updated Address / Returned Address / Returned By answer “do we have somewhere better to send this?”, the table is shaped for the next action, not just the failure log.
    2. In-House and Vendor tabs preserved even in failure. The liability split stays legible at every state, including the bad ones.
    How far this was validated

    What I tested, and what I didn’t

    I want to be precise about this. I pressure-tested the model with the three people who own this workflow end to end: a Business Analyst, a Product Manager, and the Director of Product. It hasn’t been tested with the agents who actually click Send on a live call. That’s the limit, and it shapes what I’d do next.

    What I did test

    I walked the end-to-end model with the BA, PM and Director, scripting the worst real cases: an account under dispute, a missing validation notice, a vendor send that can't be recalled. I reshaped gates wherever they flagged a path the policy wouldn't allow.

    Where it stops

    Internal experts are close to the problem, but they aren't the user. A model that satisfies the people who wrote the policy can still trip an agent working under call-time pressure. So I treat this as validated in principle, not in the field.

    What I'd test next

    Moderated walkthroughs with real agents and reviewers on the worst cases, then a controlled pilot measuring review-to-dispatch time and pre-dispatch error rate. Those two numbers would show whether the gates help more than they cost.

    State 04 · Dispatched, tradeoffs & impact

    What the gates cost, and what they bought

    Governance is never free. This section names what the design trades away, and how each cost was contained.

    Cost · Slower sends

    A gated lifecycle is slower than a button. Vendor communications now wait for human approval.

    Mitigation

    Friction is spent only where dispatch is irreversible. In-house and low-risk flows stay near-instant, so the average send got safer without getting uniformly slower.

    Cost · Reviewer bottleneck

    Concentrating approval in a queue concentrates load on reviewers.

    Mitigation

    Automated completeness checks (A05) clear the mechanical failures before human review, so reviewers judge content and context, not missing attachments.

    Cost · Workaround pressure

    Heavy process invites side channels, the email someone sends “just this once” outside the system.

    Mitigation

    The governed path was made the convenient path: guided initiation, ready templates, and one place to track everything make the system faster than the workaround.

    Getting it shipped

    Designing the gate was the easy part

    A gate that slows people down is a political object as much as a UI one. The model only shipped because three different rooms agreed to it, and each had a reason to say no.

    Resistance · Operations

    "Every gate is a second an agent isn't closing an account." Ops read the review queue as a productivity tax on people already under call-time pressure.

    How I won it

    I showed that in-house and low-risk sends stay near-instant, and only the irreversible vendor path waits. The average send got safer without getting uniformly slower.

    Resistance · Compliance

    The opposite pull: compliance wanted a human gate on every channel, the safest-looking option on paper.

    How I won it

    I argued that uniform friction is self-defeating. If every send is gated, people learn to treat the gate as noise and route around it. Calibrating the gate to liability is what keeps the heavy one credible.

    Resistance · Engineering

    The full lifecycle was a large build, and there was pressure to ship a confirmation dialog and call it governance.

    How I won it

    I sequenced it. Make invalid states unreachable in the configurator first, the highest-leverage and lowest-cost win, then layer review and the Mail Manager on top.

    Honest critique

    What I'd revisit

    Every screen above could be designed another way; the craft notes argue why these versions won given the constraints, not that they're final. Three things I'd take another pass at. The Send Communication modal is dense, and the template preview competes for space; a side-panel or expandable preview would breathe better, though the modal's preserved account context mattered more at the time. The tile dashboard reads perfectly at five states but won't scale past eight; a left-rail queue list with live counts is its likely evolution. And the queues lack bulk actions. That was deliberate at v1 to keep every approval individually attributed, but an audit-safe bulk pattern (one actor, many items, one ledger entry each) is the obvious next iteration.

    I prototyped that next iteration → Part II

    Closing the loop on the opening risk

    What it changed

    This shipped without a formal measurement program, so I won't claim percentages I can't defend. What I can stand behind are the guarantees the design makes structurally. They were true the day it shipped, independent of any dashboard.

    Guaranteed by design

    0

    unreviewed vendor communications can reach dispatch, the gate is structural, not procedural.

    1 surface

    replaced fragmented channel views with a single lifecycle dashboard for operations and compliance.

    Audit-ready

    every communication carries its state history, documents, and approver, evidence by default.

    State 05 · Closed, reflection

    What designing governance taught me about friction

    This project changed how I think about enterprise UX. In regulated work, the instinct to strip out all friction is wrong. The skill is placing it: keeping friction where the consequences are irreversible, and removing it everywhere else so the heavy gates stay credible.

    The version I’m proud of makes the wrong action harder to take than the right one. That was the whole goal.

    It also taught me to design the model before the screens. The whiteboard state machine did more for this product’s coherence than any single interface decision, every screen that followed was just that model, made visible at a different point in its life.

    Velosidy · Communication governance · Case study Part II, the redesign prototype → Reviewed & cleared
